records.ety: [OE. contenance, countenaunce, demeanor, composure, F. contenance demeanor, fr. L. continentia continence, LL. also, demeanor, fr. L. continere to hold together, repress, contain. See Contain, and cf. Continence.]

defs: Appearance or expression of the face; look; aspect; mien.
quotes: So spake the Son, and into terror changed His countenance.
In countenance somewhat doth resemble you.

defs: Approving or encouraging aspect of face; hence, favor, good will, support; aid; encouragement.
quotes: Thou hast made him . . . glad with thy countenance.
This is the magistrate's peculiar province, to give countenance to piety and virtue, and to rebuke vice.
The election being done, he made countenance of great discontent thereat.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Countenanced (-nanst); p. pr. & vb. n. Countenancing.]

defs: To encourage; to favor; to approve; to aid; to abet.
quotes: This conceit, though countenanced by learned men, is not made out either by experience or reason.
Error supports custom, custom countenances error.

defs: To make a show of; to pretend.
quotes: Which to these ladies love did countenance.
